input.prompt:
I have a new C# MCP server project. I need to implement a tool class that
wraps a REST API using HttpClient. The tools should follow MCP SDK conventions
with proper attributes for LLM discovery. Show me the tool class, Program.cs
with stdio transport, and explain how DI works for MCP tools.

data.content:
I have a new C# MCP server project. I need to implement a tool class that
wraps a REST API using HttpClient. The tools should follow MCP SDK conventions
with proper attributes for LLM discovery. Show me the tool class, Program.cs
with stdio transport, and explain how DI works for MCP tools.

<current_datetime>2026-05-05T00:47:26.666Z</current_datetime>

I have a new C# MCP server project. I need to implement a tool class that
wraps a REST API using HttpClient. The tools should follow MCP SDK conventions
with proper attributes for LLM discovery. Show me the tool class, Program.cs
with stdio transport, and explain how DI works for MCP tools.

input.initialPrompt:
I have a new C# MCP server project. I need to implement a tool class that
wraps a REST API using HttpClient. The tools should follow MCP SDK conventions
with proper attributes for LLM discovery. Show me the tool class, Program.cs
with stdio transport, and explain how DI works for MCP tools.

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-6e0c663407164d8fb71e9267abc2a9dd && mkdir -p McpApiServer && cd McpApiServer && dotnet new console --no-restore -q 2>/dev/null; echo "---done---"

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to create a C# MCP (Model Context Protocol) server project with:
1. A tool class wrapping a REST API using HttpClient
2. Program.cs with stdio transport
3. An explanation of how DI (Dependency Injection) works for MCP tools

Let me provide a complete example using the official MCP C# SDK (`ModelContextProtocol` NuGet package). I'll create a working example.

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-6e0c663407164d8fb71e9267abc2a9dd && mkdir -p McpApiServer && cd McpApiServer && dotnet new console --no-restore -q 2>/dev/null; echo "---done---"

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-6e0c663407164d8fb71e9267abc2a9dd && mkdir -p McpApiServer && cd McpApiServer && dotnet new console --no-restore -q 2>/dev/null; echo \"---done---\"", "description": "Create new console project", "initial_wait": 15}

toolArgs.command: cd /tmp/sv-6e0c663407164d8fb71e9267abc2a9dd && mkdir -p McpApiServer && cd McpApiServer && dotnet new console --no-restore -q 2>/dev/null; echo "---done---"

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-6e0c663407164d8fb71e9267abc2a9dd/McpApiServer && dotnet add package ModelContextProtocol --version 0.2.0-preview.1 -q 2>/dev/null && dotnet add package Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting -q 2>/dev/null && dotnet add package Microsoft.Extensions.Http -q 2>/dev/null; echo "---done---"
arguments.description: Add MCP SDK and hosting packages

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
'-q' was not matched. Did you mean one of the following?
-v
-f
-n
-s

Description:
  Add a NuGet package reference to the project.

Usage:
  dotnet add [<PROJECT | FILE>] package <packageId> [options]

Arguments:
  <PROJECT | FILE>  The project file or C# file-based app to operate on. If a file is not specified, the command will 
                    search the current directory for a project file. [default: 
                    /tmp/sv-6e0c663407164d8fb71e9267abc2a9dd/McpApiServer/]
  <packageId>       Package reference in the form of a package identifier like 'Newtonsoft.Json' or package identifier 
                    and version separated by '@' like 'Newtonsoft.Json@13.0.3'.

Options:
  -v, --version <VERSION>            The version of the package to add.
  -f, --framework <FRAMEWORK>        Add the reference only when targeting a specific framework.
  -n, --no-restore                   Add the reference without performing restore preview and compatibility check. 
                                     [default: False]
  -s, --source <SOURCE>              The NuGet package source to use during the restore.
  --package-directory <PACKAGE_DIR>  The directory to restore packages to.
  --interactive                      Allows the command to stop and wait for user input or action (for example to 
                                     complete authentication). [default: False]
  --prerelease                       Allows prerelease packages to be installed. [default: False]
  --project <project>                The project file to operate on. If a file is not specified, the command will 
                                     search the current directory for one.
  --file <file>                      The file-based app to operate on.
  -?, -h, --help                     Show command line help.

---done---
<exited with exit code 0>
